entry.title: Escaping V8 Sandbox via JIT Spraying by Turbofan: Part 2 (10.0.52 <= V8 < 11.7.154)
entry.content:
Because of the external code space enabled in the patch for part 1, the execution flow of the function compiled by Turbofan has been slightly changed. Still, it’s possible to hijack the execution flow if we have limited exploitation primitives within the V8 sandbox.

Setup

  • Ubuntu 20.04
  • 4512c6eb7189c21f39420ddf8d9ff4f05a4a39b4 (Jul 11, 2023)

Run setup.zsh in the working directory.

Execution Flow of Function Compiled by Turbofan

JSFunction class has an accessor named code that points to a Code object.

Code class has a pointer named instruction_start that points to the address where the instructions of the function begin.

When the compiled function is called, the function call handler gets the instruction_start pointer and jumps to that address.

Exploitation

Hijacking execution flow

The Code object is inside the V8 sandbox, so we can overwrite the instruction_start pointer with an arbitrary address which we want rip to jump to, using the exploitation primitives.

Bisection

[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64 (Jan 24, 2022)
Revert “[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64” (Jan 24, 2022)
[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64 (Jan 26, 2022)
Revert “[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64” (Jan 26, 2022)
[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64 (Jan 27, 2022)
Revert “[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64” (Jan 28, 2022)
[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64 (Jan 28, 2022)

Patch

[sandbox] Enable code pointer sandboxing (Jul 11, 2023)
Revert “[sandbox] Enable code pointer sandboxing” (Jul 11, 2023)
Reland “[sandbox] Enable code pointer sandboxing” (Jul 11, 2023)

entry.title: Escaping V8 Sandbox via JIT Spraying by Turbofan: Part 1 (V8 < 10.0.52)
entry.content:
In this post, I will introduce an exploitation technique that allows us to escape the V8 sandbox via JIT spraying by Turbofan. Using this technique, we can achieve arbitrary code execution starting from limited exploitation primitives inside the sandbox.

Setup

  • Ubuntu 20.04
  • 17f686b1b7a6550b3a6e20a4f4455c39f6cebd05 (Jan 28, 2022)

Place setup.zsh and sandbox.diff in the working directory, then run setup.zsh.

sandbox.diff is identical to the commit that introduced the memory corruption API, which allows us to implement limited exploitation primitives within the V8 sandbox.

Execution Flow of Function Compiled by Turbofan

Functions in JavaScript are managed by the JSFunction class.

JSFunction has an accessor named code that points to a Code object.

We can follow the execution flow by setting a read watchpoint on the code field of the function.

At this point, rcx holds the address of the Code object.

The value at rcx + 0x1b indicates the flags field.

Comparing the flags with 0x20000000 tests whether IsOffHeapTrampoline, which is the 30th bit of the flags, is true.

If IsOffHeapTrampoline is false, the function calculates the start address of the instructions of the function by simply adding 0x3f to the address of the Code object, and jumps to the instructions.

If we can overwrite the code field of the function, we can divert the execution flow to an arbitrary address within the V8 sandbox.

Optimizing CreateArrayLiteral

We can see the generated bytecode for f() with --print-bytecode execution flag.

CreateArrayLiteral is a bytecode instruction used to create a new array in a function. The function contains an ArrayBoilerplateDescription object, which serves as a template for the array created in the function, in its constant pool.

constant_elements is a FixedArrayBase object (FixedArray, FixedInt64Array, FixedDoubleArray, …) depending on the type of elements of the array.

CreateArrayLiteral() copies constant_elements to copied_elements_values and uses them to create a new array.

When Turbofan compiles the function, the CreateArrayLiteral instruction is converted to a JSCreateLiteralArray node, which also references the boilerplate object.

JSCreateLiteralArray node is lowered in TypedLoweringPhase.

JSCreateLowering::ReduceJSCreateLiteralArrayOrObject() calls JSCreateLowering::TryAllocateFastLiteral() to optimize the allocation of an array in case feedback is sufficient.

JSCreateLowering::TryAllocateFastLiteral() calls JSCreateLowering::TryAllocateFastLiteralElements() to optimize the allocation of the elements array.

JSCreateLowering::TryAllocateFastLiteralElements() gets boilerplate_elements from the boilerplate object.

If boilerplate_elements is a FixedDoubleArray object, it adds NumberConstant nodes for all elements.

These constants are lowered in SimplifiedLoweringPhase.

When RepresentationSelector::VisitNode encounters a StoreElement node, it calls RepresentationSelector::ProcessInput() for each input node.

For the elements of the array, RepresentationSelector::ProcessInput() gets into RepresentationSelector::ConvertInput() → RepresentationChanger::GetRepresentationFor() → RepresentationChanger::GetFloat64RepresentationFor().

RepresentationChanger::GetFloat64RepresentationFor() creates a new Float64Constant node.

At the last in RepresentationSelector::ConvertInput(), the old NumberConstant node is replaced with the new Float64Constant node.

The Float64Constant node is handled by InstructionSelectorT::VisitConstant() in InstructionSelectionPhase.

InstructionSelectorT::VisitConstant() calls OperandGeneratorT::DefineAsConstant().

OperandGeneratorT::DefineAsConstant() calls InstructionSequence::AddConstant().

InstructionSequence::AddConstant() inserts a pair of register and constant into constants_.

This pair is assembled to the mov instruction by CodeGenerator::AssembleMove() in AssembleCodePhase.

The constant is inserted into the assembly code in the form of uint64_t.

In conclusion, we can insert arbitrary 8-byte floating-point numbers, which can be potentially used for JIT spraying, into the assembly code of the function.

Exploitation

Spraying shellcode

We can execute up to 8-byte shellcode by inserting it into the assembly code of the function compiled by Turbofan and moving rip to where the shellcode lies by overwriting the code pointer of the JSFunction object.

However, it’d be impossible to do what we want, for example, execve("/bin/sh", 0, 0), with only 8 bytes of assembly code. Therefore, we must chain several shellcode segments using relative jmp instructions. The distances between the floating-point numbers in the code are consistent, so we can make each segment jump to the next one by constructing each one with a 6-byte shellcode and a 2-byte jmp instruction.

Hijacking execution flow

If the IsOffHeapTrampoline flag of the Code object is set, rip takes an unexpected path. In this case, we fail to divert the execution flow of the function to the intended path. rcx + 0x1b would be in the middle of the assembly instructions, thus it’s not a good idea to attempt to control the value there. Instead, we can add a dummy element that changes the offset of the shellcode from the instruction start to the array constructed in the Turbofan-compiled function, and hope it passes the test.

Patch

[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64 (Jan 24, 2022)
Revert “[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64” (Jan 24, 2022)
[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64 (Jan 26, 2022)
Revert “[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64” (Jan 26, 2022)
[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64 (Jan 27, 2022)
Revert “[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64” (Jan 28, 2022)
[ext-code-space] Enable external code space on x64 and desktop arm64 (Jan 28, 2022)

entry.title: CVE-2019-5782: Improper Typing for arguments.length by Turbofan Leading to RCE (7.2.41 <= V8 < 7.3.1)
entry.content:
In this post, I will analyze and exploit CVE-2019-5782, a bug caused by the improper implementation of Turbofan, a JIT compiler in V8. We can exploit the mishandling of arguments.length by Turbofan to trigger out-of-bounds access and achieve arbitrary code execution.

Setup

  • Ubuntu 20.04
  • 18b28402118b7918512c3e5b6bc5c6f348d43564 (Nov 30, 2018)

Run setup.zsh in the working directory.

Typing arguments.length

In JavaScript, arguments.length implies the length of arguments array.

The maximum number of arguments of a JavaScript function is defined by Code::kMaxArguments as 0xfffe.

Thus, the type of arguments.length is predefined for Turbofan compilation by TypeCache::kArgumentsLengthType.

When Turbofan encounters arguments.length while compiling a JavaScript function, it sets the type of the node to TypeCache::kArgumentsLengthType.

Root Cause

However, we can actually pass more than 0xfffe arguments to a JavaScript function. If so, arguments.length is mistyped, eventually leading to type confusion.

Exploitation

Triggering OOB using the right shift operator

The right shift (>>) operator is interpreted as ShiftRight (or ShiftRightSmi in case the right operand is an integer) bytecode instruction.

In GraphBuilderPhase of Turbofan compilation, a SpeculativeNumberShiftRight node is built from the ShiftRight instruction.

In TyperPhase, SpeculativeNumberShiftRight is typed for the first time. arguments.length is not optimized yet at this point, so a LoadField node responsible for loading arguments.length is passed as the left operand.

The type is calculated by OperationTyper::NumberShiftRight().

It sets min to the smaller of min_lhs >> min_rhs and min_lhs >> max_rhs, and sets max to the larger of max_lhs >> min_rhs and max_lhs >> max_rhs.

As a result, the initial type of SpeculativeNumberShiftRight is set to Range(-32768, 32767).

The nodes responsible for storing arguments_map, properties, elements, arguments_length and callee are created, and JSCreateArguments is converted to a FinishRegion node by AllocationBuilder::FinishAndChange().

The output of LoadField, which is the length of the arguments object, already exists in the graph as an ArgumentsLength node.

LoadElimination::ReduceLoadField() looks for the ArgumentsLength from the graph, and replaces the current LoadField with the existing ArgumentsLength.

The logic is the same as in TyperPhase, but the type of left input node is TypeCache::kArgumentsLengthType at this time, resulting in both min and max being 0.

The result of SpeculativeNumberShiftRight is used as idx to get an element from arr.

There’s a CheckBounds node responsible for preventing out-of-bounds. It takes the index as the first input and the array length as the second, and checks whether the index is less than the length. Also in SimplifiedLoweringPhase, the CheckBounds is reduced by RepresentationSelector::VisitCheckBounds().

In case index_type.Max() is smaller than length_type.Min(), there’s no possibility of out-of-bounds, so VisitCheckBounds() calls RepresentationSelector::DeferReplacement() to remove CheckBounds.

After compilation is finished, f() takes 0x10000 arguments, but Turbofan didn’t consider it possible. The bounds check was already removed because Turbofan assumed that idx is always 0. However, idx becomes 1 this time, so we can access the out-of-bounds of arr and read the value at the location corresponding to arr[1].

Obtaining OOB array

We can access the length field of an array using OOB.

We can overwrite the length field of the array with a larger value, without extending the elements array.

Via this corrupted array, we can access elements beyond the bounds of the array, such as arr[100], which should be undefined under normal circumstances.

Getting address of arbitrary object

An array containing JavaScript objects holds their addresses in its memory.

We can place an array of objects right behind the OOB array we obtained above, and read the value stored in the array of objects via the OOB array, so that we can get the address of an arbitrary object in the form of a floating-point number.

Arbitrary address read/write

JSTypedArray has a FixedTypedArrayBase object in its elements field.

FixedTypedArrayBase has a base_pointer that points to itself, and an external_pointer that indicates the distance between the base_pointer and the actual data.

We can place a JSTypedArray right behind the OOB array we obtained above, and overwrite base_pointer and external_pointer with an arbitrary address and 0, respectively. Then, we can read the value at the address via that array, and any value we store in that array would be written to that address.

Arbitrary code execution

Escaping V8 Sandbox via Backing Store of ArrayBuffer: Part 1 (V8 < 10.0.138)

Bisection

[Build] Disable build-time untrusted code mitigations on non-Android platforms and ia32. (Oct 18, 2018)

Patch

[runtime] Reduce spread/apply call max arguments (Nov 22, 2018)
Revert “[runtime] Reduce spread/apply call max arguments” (Nov 30, 2018)
[turbofan] Relax range for arguments object length (Nov 30, 2018)

References

  • Stable Channel Update for Desktop (Tuesday, January 29, 2019) - Chrome Releases
  • Security: Tianfu CUP RCE - Chromium Issues

entry.title: Escaping V8 Sandbox via Backing Store of ArrayBuffer: Part 2 (10.0.138 <= V8 < 10.3.163)
entry.content:
Patch for part 1 enables wasm_write_protect_code_memory by default. It aims to prohibit overwriting the WebAssembly code space, but it’s not yet thorough. We can still obtain unsandboxed AAW via the backing store of ArrayBuffer and manually disable the feature by overwriting wasm_write_protect_code_memory to false at runtime. Then, we can achieve arbitrary code execution in the same manner as in part 1.

Setup

  • Ubuntu 22.04
  • 7e9715d8a955214788475dd33a16312ba4d5c3da (May 10, 2022)

Place setup.zsh and sandbox.diff in the working directory, then run setup.zsh.

sandbox.diff is identical to the commit that introduced the memory corruption API, which allows us to implement limited exploitation primitives within the V8 sandbox.

WebAssembly code protection via mprotect

CodeSpaceWriteScope::SetExecutable() calls NativeModule::RemoveWriter() to keep write permission not set while there’s no need to write anything to the WebAssembly code space.

The permission is restored by WasmCodeAllocator::MakeWritable() if necessary. This behavior prevents us from writing our shellcode to the code space as in part 1, because there won’t be write permission at the moment we attempt to write.

With --trace-wasm-native-heap execution flag enabled, we can monitor allocations and permissions of the WebAssembly code space.

However, CodeSpaceWriteScope::SetExecutable() checks FLAG_wasm_write_protect_code_memory every time it’s called. If we can overwrite the flag to false at runtime, the feature is disabled immediately, and we can achieve arbitrary code execution in the same manner as part 1.

Exploitation

Get address of FLAG_wasm_write_protect_code_memory

WasmInstanceObject has an accessor named isolate_root that points to isolate_data_.

IsolateData has external_reference_table_, which contains a pointer to the execution flag of V8.

The order of the execution flags is consistent unless the V8 version is changed.

Thus, we can reliably obtain the address of FLAG_wasm_write_protect_code_memory from the address of FLAG_trace_osr.

Disable code protection and achieve code execution

We can overwrite FLAG_wasm_write_protect_code_memory to false so that write permission of the WebAssembly code space is never removed from now on.

Afterwards, we can achieve arbitrary code execution by overwriting the code space with shellcode, just as in part 1.

Bisection

[wasm] Ship code protection via mprotect (Feb 15, 2022)

Patch

[sandbox] Enable sandboxed pointers on Desktop (May 5, 2022)
Revert “[sandbox] Enable sandboxed pointers on Desktop” (May 5, 2022)
Reland “[sandbox] Enable sandboxed pointers on Desktop” (May 6, 2022)
Revert “Reland “[sandbox] Enable sandboxed pointers on Desktop”” (May 6, 2022)
Reland “Reland “[sandbox] Enable sandboxed pointers on Desktop”” (May 10, 2022)

The above commits enabled v8_enable_sandboxed_pointers by default when v8_enable_sandbox is enabled, resulting in the backing store of ArrayBuffer being allocated inside the V8 sandbox rather than in heap memory outside the sandbox, and ArrayBuffer containing a compressed pointer to the backing store.

[sandbox] Also enable the sandbox outside of Chromium builds (Jun 17, 2022)
Revert “[sandbox] Also enable the sandbox outside of Chromium builds” (Jun 20, 2022)
Reland “[sandbox] Also enable the sandbox outside of Chromium builds” (Jun 21, 2022)
[sandbox] Disable the sandbox by default outside of Chromium builds (Jul 19, 2022)
[sandbox] Enable the sandbox by default in V8 builds (Sep 23, 2022)

v8_enable_sandbox was enabled by default for standalone V8 builds in the commits above, whereas it has already been enabled in Chromium builds since the following commit.

entry.title: Escaping V8 Sandbox via Backing Store of ArrayBuffer: Part 1 (V8 < 10.0.138)
entry.content:
In this post, I will introduce an exploitation technique that enables us to escape the V8 sandbox via the backing store of ArrayBuffer. This allows us to achieve arbitrary code execution from the limited exploitation primitives within the V8 sandbox.

Setup

  • Ubuntu 20.04
  • 0ac7e1203fcb957851887fb140dc8a41139846a5 (Feb 15, 2022)

Place setup.zsh and sandbox.diff in the working directory, then run setup.zsh.

sandbox.diff is identical to the commit that introduced the memory corruption API, which allows us to implement limited exploitation primitives within the V8 sandbox.

Backing Store of ArrayBuffer

The ArrayBuffer object in JavaScript is managed by the JSArrayBuffer class.

JSArrayBuffer has a backing_store pointer that points to the memory holding the actual value stored in the ArrayBuffer.

We have to use TypedArray or DataView to access the values stored in an ArrayBuffer. For example, we can store an 8-byte value with DataView.prototype.setBigUint64(), which is managed by DataViewPrototypeSetBigUint64().

DataViewPrototypeSetBigUint64() gets into DataViewSet() → StoreDataViewBigInt() → StoreDataView64().

StoreDataView64() stores the value in the backing store one byte at a time.

If we can overwrite the backing store pointer of ArrayBuffer with an arbitrary address, we can access that address to read from or write to.

Execution Flow of WebAssembly Function

When a WebAssembly module is constructed, code_space for the WebAssembly code is allocated.

jump_table is created at the very front of the code space. The jump table determines the execution flow of functions in the WebAssembly module.

The code space permission is set to RWX because compiled WebAssembly code has to be written to and executed in this space.

test.wat defines a WebAssembly module containing three functions, all of which are exported to JavaScript.

The jump table consists of jmp instructions, each of which corresponds to a function in the module.

We can access the exported functions with WebAssembly.Instance.prototype.exports.

Each function is managed by the JSFunction class, like any other function in JavaScript.

JSFunction has an accessor named code pointing to CodeDataContainer.

CodeDataContainer has an accessor named code_entry_point that points to where the instruction pointer moves when the function is called.

At this point, rdi holds the address of the Function object. The instructions from here are responsible for getting the call target corresponding to the function called.

Eventually, the instruction pointer moves to the third entry of the jump table. It corresponds to f3(), which is the third function in the module.

Exploitation

Get address of WebAssembly code space

WasmInstanceObject has an accessor named jump_table_start that points to the jump table of the corresponding WebAssembly module. The jump table is located at the very front of the WebAssembly code space, so the address of the jump table is identical to that of the code space.

We can obtain the address of the WasmInstanceObject using the addrof() primitive, and read the value stored in the jump_table_start field to get the address of the code space.

Write shellcode to WebAssembly code space

We can obtain the address of an ArrayBuffer using the addrof() primitive and overwrite the backing_store field with an arbitrary 8-byte address that points to where we want to read from or write to. The address doesn’t have to be inside the V8 sandbox, so that we can perform unsandboxed AAR/AAW via this corrupted ArrayBuffer.

Using the unsandboxed AAW, we can write our shellcode to the WebAssembly code space. The code space has both write and execute permissions, allowing us to execute our shellcode later.

Execute shellcode

Calling the exported function moves the instruction pointer to the WebAssembly jump table, where our shellcode lies.

Patch

[wasm] Ship code protection via mprotect (Feb 15, 2022)
